LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE DEC-14-2021 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE, ROB BONTA, AND DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE BUREAU OF FIREARMS' DEMURRER, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEMURRER IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS.) ORDER SIGNED. SEPARATE JUDGMENT SIGNED. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER, JR.; CLERK: SEAN KANE; COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR NO. 8600, EMAIL: MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM. (302/RBU)

calendar.matters:
Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer By Defendants To Plaintiff?S Petition; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support; Payment For Court Reporter Fee
Off Calendar Dec-14-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE, ROB BONTA, and DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE BUREAU OF FIREARMS' Demurrer.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant California Department of Justice, Rob Bonta, Department of Justice Bureau of Firearms' (the "Department") Demurrer to Plaintiff's Petition is sustained without leave to amend.

A demurrer may be sustained where the complaint fails to state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 430.10(e).) Thus, concerning the legal sufficiency of a pleading, the sole issue on demurrer is whether the facts pleaded, if true, state a valid cause of action, i.e., if the complaint pleads facts that would entitle the plaintiff to relief. (Garcetti v. Superior Court (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 1533, 1547; Limandri v. Judkins (1997) 52 Cal.App.4th 326, 339.)

Plaintiff seeks to remove a 5250-hold reported by a mental health facility in order to apply for employment as a patrolman with the San Francisco Police Department. (See Petition, pp. 1-2.) In effect, Plaintiff is seeking a writ of mandate. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1085.) A writ of "mandate will lie only if there is (1) a clear and present duty on the part of the respondent and (2) a clear, present and beneficial right in the Plaintiff to the performance of that duty. [Citation.]" (Kaiser Foundation Hospitals v. Belshe (1997) 54 Cal.App.4th 1547, 1558; see Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1085.)

In this case, the Department merely receives information from medical facilities about a patient who is placed on a 5250 hold, and then provides that information to the federal government. (Welf. & Inst. Code sec. 8103(g)(2); see also Penal Code sec. 28220(b) ["The Department of Justice shall participate in the National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS), as described in subsection (t) of Section 922 of Title 18 of the United States Code"].) As such, the Department has no statutory duty or authority to remove the 5250-hold reported by a mental health facility. Thus, as a matter of law, a writ of mandate is not available to Plaintiff. (See California State Employees' Assn. v. Cory (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 888, 895 ["A writ of mandate will not issue to compel performance of an act not within the power of the person to whom the writ issues"], citation omitted.) The Court denies Plaintiff's argument that under section 8103(f), the Department has the burden to correct inaccurate reports. (Opp. pg. 2.) Defendant correctly points out, section 8103(f) involves a five-year firearms prohibition for patients admitted to a mental health facility for a 72-hour period under section 5150, while this case involves Plaintiff's involuntary treatment under section 5220. (See Petition, pp. 1-2.)

Order signed. Separate judgment signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/RBU)
